rules.name: auto_trigger_patterns
rules.overview:
Partners often ask: "Is there a way to auto-trigger guardrails on every query without explicit wiring?" Yes — three patterns, depending on the agent's topology:
1. HTTP middleware — for polyglot agents behind HTTP 2. Python decorator — for monolithic Python agents 3. Framework callback — for LangChain / LangGraph / similar
None of these require changes to the SDK. They're consumer patterns built on top of the existing evaluate() API.
Read this when the partner says: "I want to add guardrails without modifying every endpoint of my agent."

branch.question: What language is the agent's request handler in?

leaf.condition: Python, plain Flask/FastAPI app with N endpoints
Mix of services in a mesh

pre_hook_vs_post_hook.overview: Every pattern has TWO attach points. Partners get confused about how output-blocking works with auto-trigger; address this head-on.

pre_hook.on_block: Skip the agent entirely. Return the fallback message directly.
pre_hook.cost: Cheap — 1 guardrail call, no LLM cost incurred.

post_hook.when: Fires AFTER the agent has generated output.
post_hook.on_block: Replace the agent's output with the fallback message before returning to the caller.
post_hook.cost: Expensive — 1+ guardrail calls PLUS one full LLM call you can't refund. The LLM cost is sunk regardless of the post-check outcome.

pre_hook_vs_post_hook.implication: Catch what you can at the input stage. Reserve output-stage checks for things that ONLY the output can reveal — PII leaks from the LLM, hallucinations vs context, profanity in generated text, RAG faithfulness. Don't duplicate input-side safety on output unless there's a specific reason.

pattern.name: http_middleware
pattern.suitability: Most universal. Works for any language with HTTP middleware. Drop-in: agent code is unchanged.
pattern.pre_post_mechanics: Both pre and post live in the SAME middleware function. - Pre = inspect request body BEFORE call_next(request). - Post = read response body AFTER call_next(request), evaluate, optionally replace with JSONResponse(fallback) on Block.

pattern.name: python_decorator
pattern.suitability: Cleanest retrofit for monolithic Python agents. Wraps a single function — agent code is unchanged except for the @guard line.
pattern.pre_post_mechanics: The decorator wraps the function: - Pre = run BEFORE the wrapped function call. On Block, return fallback WITHOUT invoking the function. - Post = run AFTER the wrapped function returns. On Block, substitute the fallback for the returned value.
pattern.reference_file: .bob/reference-payloads/decorator_example.py — self-contained ~100 lines partners copy into their codebase.
pattern.not_shipped_in_sdk: The decorator is intentionally a bob-mode reference, not an SDK feature. Partners copy + adapt. Revisit shipping in the SDK if 2+ partners request it.

pattern.name: framework_callback
pattern.suitability: For agents built on LangChain / LangGraph / similar frameworks. Hooks into the framework's existing callback system.

pre_post_mechanics_critical_limitation.pre: on_chain_start callback fires with the chain's inputs. Raising an exception from the callback aborts the chain — partner catches that exception and converts to a Block response. Clean pattern.
pre_post_mechanics_critical_limitation.post: STOCK LANGCHAIN CALLBACKS ARE OBSERVATIONAL — they can't replace outputs. Workaround: wrap the chain in a RunnableLambda post-processor that re-evaluates the result and substitutes the fallback. This is the canonical LangChain pattern for output guardrails; document the asymmetry so partners aren't surprised.

pattern.reference_file: .bob/reference-payloads/langchain_callback.py — working example with simple LangChain chain.

choke_point.name: Retrieval quality
choke_point.fits_in: LangChain framework callback (on_retriever_end); middleware can't insert here without code structure cooperation

four_choke_points_mapped_to_pre_post.note: Auto-trigger can't automatically insert choke point 2 unless the agent's code is split into retrieval + generation phases. LangChain handles this naturally because it has separate Runnables for retrieval and LLM call. Monolithic Python agents need explicit wiring for retrieval-stage checks.

stateless_requirement.rule: Build the GuardrailsEvaluator ONCE at app/worker startup. Share across requests. NEVER instantiate per-request.
stateless_requirement.reason: Registry build does ~28 SDK object constructions plus an LLMJudge build (network-y on first use). Per-request instantiation adds ~1-3 s of overhead per request — defeats the purpose of auto-trigger.

failure_mode_policy.description: What happens when the guardrails service is degraded (timeout, 5xx)?

fail_open.semantics: Allow the request to pass when guardrails fails. Log the failure for ops review.
fail_open.use_when: Availability is more important than safety enforcement (e.g. internal tooling, low-stakes use cases).

fail_closed.semantics: Refuse the request when guardrails fails. Return a generic "service unavailable" message.
fail_closed.use_when: Compliance/safety is non-negotiable (regulated industries, customer-facing high-stakes content).

failure_mode_policy.implementation: Wrap the ev.evaluate() call in try/except. On exception, apply the partner's chosen policy. This is consistent across all three trigger patterns.

anti_patterns.anti_pattern: Building an evaluator per request — cold-start cost (registry build is several seconds).
Running LLM-judge metrics on every request without sampling — token cost will blow up.
Putting output-stage RAG metrics in input-stage middleware — impossible (output doesn't exist yet).
Trying to block output via stock LangChain callbacks — won't work. Use RunnableLambda wrapping instead.
Mixing trigger patterns in the same agent — pick ONE (middleware OR decorator OR callback) and stick with it for consistency and debuggability.
